data.title: Who is creating this localized frenzy for altcoins?
data.description:
Author: Nancy

The continuous rise over the past few days has suddenly enlivened the long-dormant altcoin market. Some tokens have seen consecutive surges, even experiencing violent price increases of several times or even dozens of times in a short period, seemingly signaling the restart of the altcoin season.

However, this is not a signal of a full-scale recovery in altcoins, but rather a localized frenzy dominated by a few strong tokens.

The era of a broad-based rally is over; strong tokens take over the altcoin market.

The altcoin market has not witnessed the broad-based rally that the market had long anticipated.

According to data from CoinGecko tracking the top 1000 tokens by market capitalization, the top 30 performing tokens over the past 7 days saw an average increase of 153.5%, significantly outperforming mainstream assets such as Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Solana. These gains were primarily concentrated in memes, inscriptions, AI, and infrastructure sectors. However, further analysis reveals that this altcoin rally was not evenly distributed, but rather dominated by a very small number of exceptionally performing assets. The top 10 gainers all exceeded 100%, with RAVE, Binance Life, and ORDI leading the pack with gains of 1596.2%, 307.2%, and 265.4% respectively. The remaining 20 tokens saw relatively moderate performance, mainly concentrated in the 40%-100% range, averaging around 65.6%. In other words, a few top performers drove the overall performance. Trading volume data also confirms this. Although overall trading volume has rebounded somewhat, funds haven't flowed evenly but are concentrated on a few tokens. In the past 24 hours, the average trading volume for these tokens was approximately $140 million, with ORDI, BIO, and BASED alone accounting for nearly 70% of the trading volume, demonstrating a strong concentration of funds. If we break it down further, the assets that have risen in this round can be roughly divided into two categories: One category consists of projects that have just reached new highs and are in a strong uptrend. Twelve projects have reached new highs within a month, with most breaking their all-time price records in the last two days. These tokens have good liquidity and high market attention, but have already accumulated a large amount of profit-taking, and their current prices have retreated by an average of about 29.5% from their peak. Once new funds weaken or sentiment shifts, a sharp correction is likely. The other category consists of deeply oversold rebound assets. Thirteen tokens have been away from their all-time highs for more than a year, with the longest span exceeding four years, and an average maximum drop of 95.4%. Because the actual circulating market capitalization of these projects has shrunk significantly, only a small amount of capital is needed to quickly drive up the price, with an average increase of 104.4% in the recent rebound. From a market capitalization perspective, most cryptocurrencies currently have a market capitalization concentrated in the range of $20 million to $80 million. These low-to-mid-market-cap projects, due to their smaller circulating supply, exhibit significantly stronger price elasticity under the same amount of capital inflow compared to high-market-cap projects, making them more prone to rapid price surges. Looking at the eight projects with market capitalizations exceeding $100 million, the average increase is approximately 340%, indicating that these projects had relatively low initial market capitalizations, allowing them to achieve such strong gains. However, as market capitalization is rapidly inflated, achieving similar increases will become significantly more difficult. Limited Rebound Potential, Altcoins Enter an Era of Differentiation Although altcoin prices and trading volumes have shown signs of recovery, given the overall insufficient liquidity in the crypto market and Bitcoin's continued dominance, the rebound potential may be relatively limited. CoinGlass's altcoin seasonal index shows a current index of 36, indicating that the overall market has not yet experienced a broad-based altcoin season, with most tokens underperforming Bitcoin and funds concentrated in a few strong performers. Meanwhile, CoinGlass's Fear & Greed Index, a barometer of crypto market sentiment, shows a current index of 22, placing it in the fear zone. This low level reflects cautious sentiment among market participants, indicating an overall oversold or pessimistic state. The fear zone has historically occurred approximately 30.68% of the time, a common characteristic during bear markets in crypto, suggesting that the current rebound is not yet based on a strong emotional resonance. More importantly, the current altcoin rally is still occurring during a Bitcoin-dominated phase. According to CoinGecko data, Bitcoin currently holds a 56.8% market share in the crypto market, while Ethereum holds 10.7%. Until Bitcoin's absolute market share declines significantly, altcoins remain more of a rotating asset rather than a leading trend. This round of altcoin gains may be driven by the following key factors: First, easing geopolitical risks. Easing geopolitical risks have provided significant macroeconomic support for this altcoin rebound. Recent signs of easing tensions between the US and Iran have significantly reduced market risk premiums, driving a collective recovery in global risk assets. As a high-beta asset, the crypto market is highly sensitive to changes in geopolitical sentiment. After risk aversion eased, investor risk appetite rebounded, and some profit-taking funds rotated from mainstream assets like Bitcoin to more volatile altcoins, amplifying the upward surge of some strong performers. Secondly, there was profit-taking rotation. Bitcoin's recent sustained strength, approaching $75,000, has yielded substantial returns for early investors. As Bitcoin entered a period of high-level consolidation and its upward momentum slowed, some profit-taking funds began to flow out, shifting to altcoins with smaller market capitalizations and greater potential for growth, seeking higher returns. In particular, many established altcoins were in severely oversold territory; any liquidity replenishment could trigger a rapid rebound, further amplified by the derivatives market, pushing prices even higher. However, it's important to note that the rebound in these assets relies primarily on sentiment recovery or external catalysts, rather than fundamental improvements. Thirdly, there was significant market manipulation by large investors. The violent surges in a few cryptocurrencies are attributed to the concentrated control of tokens by project teams or market makers. These assets have extremely low liquidity, making their prices easily manipulated; they are essentially more akin to liquidity games than free market pricing. For example: RAVE had over 90% of its supply concentrated in three wallets suspected to be controlled by a team or internal entities called Gnosis Safe. By inducing short sellers to build positions, the supply tightened, causing a short squeeze and leading to a price surge. Following this sharp rise, the market immediately saw large-scale selling pressure and whale dumping. SIREN was also observed to have experienced intense market manipulation after market makers regained control of over 93% of the tokens, resulting in a significant price increase. During the surge in Meme's Binance Life token, its controlling group continuously increased its holdings, at one point exceeding 22%. Thirdly, narrative and news-driven catalysts. Factors such as funding progress, sector rotation, and the driving effect of popular concepts like AI provide short-term momentum for price increases, but their sustainability and upside potential are limited, with the overall trend being more transaction-driven than trend-driven. It's important to note that among tens of millions of altcoins, only a very small number are currently capable of truly driving prices up. Bitwise's Chief Investment Officer, Matt Hougan, recently pointed out that the traditional altcoin season with widespread price increases may have ended, and the future will see a more differentiated and unconventional altcoin cycle. CryptoQuant analyst Darkfost also stated that with approximately 47 million cryptocurrencies currently issued globally, this massive quantity directly leads to severe liquidity dilution, making altcoins increasingly vulnerable. Overall, while a few strong altcoins amplify the market effect, most assets remain in a liquidity vacuum. Investors should remain vigilant and wary of profit-taking pressure, manipulation by major players, and sharp fluctuations caused by low liquidity.

data.title: Has the Year of Blockchain Arrived on Wall Street? From Regulatory Crackdown to Compliance and Coexistence
data.description:
One ​​bounce does not make a bull. The current price is still anchored within a structured range of Realized Price ($54.4k) and True Market Mean ($78.4k). Until it firmly establishes itself above the key resistance level of $78.4k, the market's return will remain tentative.

Crypto Market Summary

During this bi-weekly observation period, Bitcoin successfully broke out of the $62.8k–$72.6k stalemate, briefly touching the $74k mark. This breakout strategically signifies that the market has moved from a geopolitically induced "forced deleveraging" phase to an "initial stabilization period."

However, as leading asset management institutions, we must remain calm: "One bounce does not make a bull." Before firmly establishing itself above the key resistance level of $78.4k, the market's return remains tentative. Glassnode data shows that support on the spot market is slowly recovering, and after continuous outflows, the 7-day moving average of US spot ETFs has turned positive. Institutional buying interest around $65k has reaffirmed the role of ETFs as a defensive center on the spot market. Macroeconomic Focus and Key Areas of Concern 1. Supply Clearing, Confidence Uncertain The market is at a crossroads of "supply clearing and uncertainty." Subsequent data analysis will demonstrate that this transition is accompanied by a sharp shift of liquidity towards high-certainty assets. The "shakeout" signal in STH-SOPR: The current 7-day average of the Short-Term Holder Payout Profit Margin (STH-SOPR) is only 0.985. This means that recently entered investors are still in a loss-making position. "So What?" Dimension: The current profit supply ratio has only recovered to about 60%, which historically belongs to the "first wave exhaustion" level at the bottom of a cycle. Only when this ratio continues to stand above 75% can the true transition from bear market recovery to the main upward wave of a bull market be confirmed. Currently, we are in the painful period of conversion of token holders from "tenants" to "owners" (Conviction Pending), and the oscillation and shakeout are a necessary process to accumulate long-term confidence. 2. Wall Street's on-chain integration with AI-Web3 Public chain ecosystems are shifting from "narrative-driven" to "industry application-driven". This transformation is no longer a simple technological iteration, but a reconstruction of governance logic.

I. Market Overview

1. Mainstream MarketSituation

The total global cryptocurrency market capitalization is $2.49 trillion, up from $2.45 trillion last week, representing a week-on-week increase of approximately 1.63%. Bitcoin's market capitalization is $1.42 trillion, accounting for 57.02% of the total cryptocurrency market capitalization. Meanwhile, stablecoins have a market capitalization of $310.3 billion, representing 12.46% of the total cryptocurrency market capitalization. The cryptocurrency fear index is 13, indicating extreme fear.

2. RWA Market Size

RWA.xyz data shows that the value of distributed assets has reached $27.35 billion. This growth is no longer a simple "mapping," but rather an industrial transformation of traditional financial infrastructure through "Staked Liquidity" (MSL) systems, represented by Midas—solving the redemption queuing problem through a pre-allocated funding layer, thus realizing the upgrade of RWA from a laboratory model to a patch for large-scale financial applications. Data growth is clearly showing a trend of "violent equalization of liquidity premiums." This on-chain migration driven by real assets forces the regulatory framework to respond defensively. II. Hot Market News The public blockchain ecosystem is shifting from "narrative-driven" to "industry application-driven." This transformation is no longer a simple technological iteration, but a reconstruction of governance logic. 1. US Stocks on Blockchain and Financial Infrastructure Restructuring: A “Reduced-Dimensional Attack” on TradFi Nasdaq Tokenization Pilot: The SEC approved DTC to conduct a three-year pilot program for tokenization services, using a “Stock Transformation Gateway.” The most crucial breakthrough is that tokenized securities share the same order book with traditional settlement, achieving seamless “hot-swappable” operation of the traditional clearing chain. S&P 500 On-Chain Deployment: S&P 500 Authorizes Trade XYZ to Launch Official Perpetual Contracts on Hyperliquid, Supporting 50x Leverage and Directly Utilizing S&P DJI Institutional-Grade Raw Data. This "Institutional-Grade Data + Decentralized Execution" model represents a game-changer for the traditional 5x8 trading system in finance. Jupiter and Pre-IPO Trading: By listing tokenized equities such as OpenAI and Anthropic, Jupiter is releasing liquidity premiums from unlisted unicorns, representing a powerful equalization of liquidity in Silicon Valley's equity market. 2. The Convergence of AI and Web3: Capital's Occupation of Sovereignty Nous Research's Valuation Benchmark: Nous Research, the team behind Hermes Agent, completed a funding round with a token valuation of $1 billion, with investors including Paradigm. This signifies that crypto capital is no longer satisfied with simple speculation, but has begun to "occupy sovereignty" over top-level open-source AI frameworks. Mining Companies Transform into HPC: Mining companies such as Bitdeer and Riot leverage their grid resource advantages to transform into high-performance AI computing, achieving strategic synergy between energy assets and cutting-edge computing power. 3. Governance Transformation and VC Survival Rules Solana's "HDB Politics": Drawing on Singapore's governance logic, Solana is forcibly transforming "floating speculators (tenants)" into "asset holders (owners)" through airdrops (such as JUP, JTO) and staking mechanisms. "Only those with assets will protect the country"—this binding of interests is the underlying logic for combating centrifugal forces within the community. VC Paradigm Thinking: As Dragonfly stated, the industry is shifting from "heroism" to rewarding "stable and sustainable contributors." Venture capital firms must align with LPs' demand for certainty (stablecoins, perpetual contracts) rather than blindly betting on disorderly innovation. III. Macroeconomic and Regulatory Dynamics US regulation is undergoing a strategic shift from "encirclement" to "rule-based survival." Recent White House actions indicate that crypto regulation is no longer just a financial issue, but also a geopolitical game for maintaining the digital sovereignty of the US dollar. 1. In-depth analysis of the Clarity Act: Policy rifts and sovereign competition. This act is a nuclear-level tool for reshaping the landscape of the "digital dollar," and is currently in a critical period of clearing the final technical hurdles: The core game and the challenge from the Association of Bankers (ABA): A compromise has been reached on the controversy over whether stablecoins should pay yields. Although a White House report claims this will not shake the foundation of bank deposits, the ABA strongly opposes this, arguing that the report has serious flaws. This open rift between institutions reflects the deep-seated fear of "liquidity disintermediation" within the traditional banking industry. Key Obstacles Cleared: Negotiations have made substantial progress in sensitive areas such as DeFi illicit finance protection measures and the Democratic Party's demand for "restrictions on senior officials (such as Trump) working in the industry." 2. Breaking the Impasse on Administrative Exemptions and State Reserves: SEC Administrative Exemptions: The SEC has explicitly guided that self-custodied wallet interfaces that do not actively solicit or guide routing choices will be exempt from brokerage registration. This provides a legal loophole for the front end of decentralized protocols to survive. North Carolina Bill 327: This bill has passed its first reading and proposes to authorize the allocation of 10% of public funds to Bitcoin. Its importance lies not only in the allocation ratio but also in its stringent state-level auditing and multi-signature cold storage standards, formally establishing Bitcoin's legal status as a "state-level strategic asset." 3. Global Geopolitics and Geofinancial Integration: The direct talks between the US and Iran scheduled for April 16 in Islamabad have provided a buffer period for market risk aversion. At the same time, the European Central Bank (ECB) insists that tokenization must be pegged to central bank currency and maintain strict interoperability, reflecting Europe's defensive regulatory stance against the fragmentation of the financial system.

data.title: The focus of competition in stablecoins is shifting.
data.description:
The discussion surrounding stablecoins has long focused on one question:Who will issue the next dominant stablecoin?

However, Circle, a stablecoin issuer, is diminishing the significance of this question itself through its recent actions in South Korea.

In his latest statement, Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire explicitly stated that there are currently no plans to launch a stablecoin for the Korean won. Meanwhile, Circle is making intensive progress in Seoul: Deepening cooperation with the exchange system; establishing connections with banks and financial groups. This combination of "avoiding token issuance and strengthening connections" essentially points to a more crucial change: the core of stablecoin competition is shifting from "issuance rights" to "system position".

South Korean Market: High Activity and Low Structure Coexist

To understand the Circle strategy, you need to first understand the structure of the South Korean market.

...p>

To understand the Circle strategy, you need to understand the structure of the South Korean market.

According to data from the crypto data agency Kaiko: South Korea accounts for approximately 30% of global crypto trading volume, with altcoins accounting for a staggering 85% of that. Bitcoin and Ethereum combined account for less than 15% of the total. Behind this data lies not just "active trading," but three deeper characteristics: Sentiment-driven: Funds are concentrated in highly volatile assets, with significant short-term speculative characteristics. Retail investor-driven: Institutional participation is rudimentary, and market depth is insufficient. Structural imbalance: Liquidity quality is lower than that of mature markets (such as Japan). This means that South Korea is not a "mature financial market," but rather a "high-volume market that has not completed its structural upgrade." And this is precisely the time when Circle entered the market. Circle's Three Key Actions Under this market structure, Circle's strategy exhibits a highly consistent direction.

1. Embedding Transaction Traffic Entry Point

Circle's expansion of its partnerships with Upbit operators Dunamu and Bithumb is not simply about business expansion, but rather about embedding USDC and related services into South Korea's core transaction infrastructure. This corresponds to a key location—the entry point for capital flow. 2. Connecting with Core Institutions of the Financial System According to reports, Allaire's meetings during this trip included Shinhan Bank, one of South Korea's largest commercial banks; KB Financial Group, a leading integrated financial holding group in South Korea; Woori Bank, South Korea's second-largest national commercial bank and the only state-owned bank; KakaoGroup, South Korea's instant messaging giant; Hashed, a South Korean cryptocurrency venture capital firm; and Coinone, South Korea's third-largest exchange. This combination has a clear target: The banking system: potential issuers. Technology Platform: User Entry Point

  • Exchange: Liquidity Core

    • Its essence is to complete the connection and binding of multiple stakeholders before the implementation of regulations. 3. Explicitly Avoiding Competition for Issuance Rights While the path to a stablecoin in South Korea remains uncertain, Circle has chosen not to participate in the issuance of a Korean won stablecoin. This decision is not conservative, but based on a clearer assessment: While the issuance rights are still under negotiation, the infrastructure needs are already established. The core conflict in South Korea: the dispute over the ownership of issuance rights. Currently, the disagreement surrounding stablecoins in South Korea centers on: some policymakers favor issuance by technology companies, while the banking system and central bank advocate for banks to lead the issuance. The essence of this conflict is: are stablecoins financial instruments or internet products?

    Until this issue is resolved:

    • The issuing entity cannot be determined

    • From Issuer to Infrastructure Provider

      Integrating the above actions, we can arrive at a clear conclusion: Circle is shifting from a "stablecoin issuer" to a "stablecoin infrastructure provider". This transformation is reflected in three aspects: Revenue Structure: From Issuance Scale to Technology and Service Capabilities Risk Structure: Avoiding Direct Regulatory Uncertainty Market Adaptation: Flexible Embedding in Different Regulatory Systems Under this model, Circle can participate regardless of who ultimately issues the stablecoin. Circle's Asia Observations Following his trip to South Korea, Jeremy Allaire also mentioned that there is a huge opportunity for a stablecoin for the renminbi, and that China may launch it within the next 3 to 5 years. This statement does not address the pathways or methods of participation; it is more of a generalization of regional trends. However, considering Circle's actual actions in South Korea, it can be understood as a more holistic observation of Asia. From the current situation, although China and South Korea have taken different paths, they exhibit several common characteristics: Stablecoins have entered the core regulatory agenda; their relationship with the traditional financial system is becoming increasingly close. The participants are becoming more diversified. In this process, the role of stablecoins is changing: from an early medium of exchange for cryptocurrencies to a broader financial infrastructure. Circle's expansion in South Korea, and its continued focus on the Chinese market, essentially point to the same thing: finding a place to embed itself within this evolving infrastructure. The changes in Circle's strategy reflect a broader trend. The future stablecoin system will likely take the form of: Coexistence of multiple sovereign currencies. Deep Integration with the Traditional Financial System In this framework, issuance rights belong to the state or licensed institutions, and technology and clearing capabilities have become new core competitive advantages. Therefore, the value of institutions like Circle is no longer limited to USDC itself, but lies in whether they can serve as a connecting and infrastructure layer between different stablecoin systems.

      The core issue is no longer who will issue the token.

      While the market is still discussing: Who will issue a stablecoin? Which country will be the first to launch it?

      While the market is still debating: Who will issue a stablecoin? Which country will be the first to launch it?

      The core issue is no longer who will issue the token.

      While the market is still debating: Who will issue a stablecoin? Which country will be the first to launch it?

      Circle has already provided another answer through its actions: What determines long-term position is not the right to publish, but whether it is embedded in the system. In the future stablecoin landscape, a structure is likely to emerge: Highly localized issuers. Highly Converged Regulation Underlying capabilities are provided by a few global institutions. In this structure: Some companies will not be at the forefront, but will be present in every transaction.

data.title: Did Circle Let $280M in Stolen USDC Slip Away? Drift Investors Sue Over Alleged Inaction in Major Exploit as CEO Jeremy Allaire Defends Legal-Only Freeze Policy
data.description:
A group of Drift Protocol investors are seeking to bring stablecoin issuer Circle to court, blaming the company for its inaction following the Drift Protocol hack.

🚨LATEST: CIRCLE HIT WITH LAWSUIT OVER $280M DRIFT EXPLOIT RESPONSE

Circle faces a class action lawsuit for its failure in freezing the stolen USDC during the Drift hack, enabling $230M to be bridged across chains, raising claims of negligence and aiding illicit transfers. pic.twitter.com/yHlU4Cgz17

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) April 17, 2026

The Drift Protocol investors are demanding answers as to why the stablecoin issuer fail to freeze hundreds of millions in stolen USDC during one of DeFi’s largest exploits.

The lawsuit, filed by law firm Gibbs Mura, centers on a narrow but crucial moment in the aftermath of the April 1 exploit. Plaintiffs argue that Circle had both the technical capability and contractual authority to freeze stolen funds, yet it didn't, allowing the attackers to move more than $230 million in USDC across chains.

The accusation was first floated by crypto sleuth ZachXBT, who claimed Circle had more than six hours to intervene before the funds were bridged from Solana to Ethereum via its infrastructure.

Circle was asleep while many millions of USDC was swapped via CCTP from Solana to Ethereum for hours from the 9 figure Drift hack during US hours.

Value was moved and nothing was done yet again.

Comes days after you froze 16+ business hot wallets incompetently which is still… pic.twitter.com/T0Xwg1HIfO

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) April 2, 2026

Yet, Circle did nothing. It only took the company many days later to freeze 16 wallets which ZachXBT claimed were unrelated to the whole hack, which the online sleuth said demonstrated the company's capability and willingness to act.

Circle’s defense: law over discretion

Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire has pushed back firmly, framing the issue as one of legal boundaries rather than technical capability. Speaking at a press conference, Allaire emphasized that the company only freezes funds when directed by law enforcement or the courts.

Acting independently, he warned, would introduce a “significant moral quandary” and risk undermining the rule of law in financial systems increasingly built on digital assets.

"If there are others that believe that Circle should just step away from what the law says and do its own, make its own decisions, I think it's a very risky proposition."

Drift protocol $DRIFT surges 19% after $150M in support from Tether and other partners for user recovery.

This comes just over two weeks after the protocol was exploited for $285M on April 1, the largest crypto hack of 2026. pic.twitter.com/UwIdteX7gd

— Jeremy (@Jeremybtc) April 16, 2026

The case has quickly evolved beyond a single exploit, raising broader questions about the role of centralized actors in decentralized ecosystems. Stablecoins like USDC rely on issuer-controlled mechanisms, including the ability to blacklist or freeze funds—features that can both protect users and introduce central points of control.

For investors, the expectation is increasingly clear: if intervention is possible, it should be exercised during major exploits. For issuers like Circle, however, acting without legal backing risks setting precedents that could be difficult to contain.

Fallout and recovery efforts

Meanwhile, Drift Protocol is attempting to stabilize operations, securing a proposed recovery package that includes up to $127.5 million in support from Tether and an additional $20 million from partners. The plan is aimed at compensating affected users and relaunching the platform.

Today, Drift is announcing a collaboration with @tether and other partners totaling up to nearly $150 million to support our commitment to a relaunch with USDT at the center, and a path to user recovery.

These funds encompass a $100M revenue-linked credit facility, an ecosystem…

— Drift (@DriftProtocol) April 16, 2026

But even as recovery efforts move forward, the legal battle against Circle could redefine expectations for how stablecoin issuers respond to future exploits.

data.title: Pre-IPO blockchain technology is gaining momentum.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

In 2026, the global IPO market showed signs of recovery, especially after the news in early April that Musk was going to launch SpaceX's IPO, which further fueled market enthusiasm. Many exchanges have launched Pre-IPO equity tokenization services, completely breaking the traditional "institutional exclusive" pattern of the Pre-IPO market.

What are the advantages of Pre-IPO? What are the thresholds? Which exchanges have launched on-chain Pre-IPO equity tokenization services? What impact will Pre-IPO on-chain have?

I. What is Pre-IPO?

Pre-IPO is the last round of market-based financing and equity transfer before a company officially launches its initial public offering (IPO), representing a "pre-listing investment opportunity." Typically, companies entering the Pre-IPO stage already possess a mature business model, stable revenue, and even profitability, and are basically ready for listing, just one step away from entering the capital market. This is the core of the attractiveness of Pre-IPO investment.

What are the advantages of Pre-IPO?

Unlike early-stage angel and Series A funding rounds, the core advantage of Pre-IPO investment lies in the expectation of "low risk and high return." Because the company's IPO is clearly anticipated, investors' funds are primarily used to optimize the company's equity structure and supplement pre-IPO working capital. After a successful IPO, investors sell their shares on the secondary market, profiting from the valuation difference before and after the IPO. This difference can often be several times or even dozens of times, which is a key reason why institutional investors have long favored Pre-IPO investments. Specifically, Pre-IPO investments have the following characteristics: Discounted Returns: Pre-IPO stock pricing is significantly lower than the publicly disclosed valuation. Limited Supply: Scarcity and uniqueness drive demand. Brand Assets: Gaining investment opportunities before giants like SpaceX go public. Lower Volatility: Private valuations are unaffected by market sentiment fluctuations. Seizing the opportunity: Profiting before Wall Street offers investment opportunities. However, in traditional financial markets, Pre-IPO has always been the "exclusive track" for institutional investors and high-net-worth individuals. On the one hand, the investment threshold for Pre-IPO is extremely high, with minimum investment amounts often reaching millions or even tens of millions of yuan, excluding the vast majority of ordinary investors. On the other hand, the transfer of Pre-IPO equity is mainly conducted through private equity funds and private transfers, and ordinary investors lack both the relevant resources and the qualifications required by regulators to be "accredited investors." The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) requires accredited investors to have an annual income of over $200,000, net assets of over $1 million (excluding the primary residence), or hold a professional license. Traditional investment channels require substantial financial strength—the minimum investment amount for venture capital funds is $1 million to $10 million, and the minimum investment amount for private equity funds is $1 million to $25 million.

II. The Rise of Pre-IPO On-Chain Trading on Crypto Exchanges

Since 2026, many top unicorns, including SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, have successively signaled their intention to IPO. SpaceX's IPO, scheduled for mid-June, is poised to become the largest IPO in history, with a peak valuation of $2 trillion.

Amid this IPO frenzy, an even more disruptive change is emerging—Pre-IPO. Leading exchanges including Binance, OKX, Gate.io, and Bitget all have corresponding product plans. Judging from the current product forms launched by various platforms, "Pre-IPO on-chain trading" is not a single model.

Binance: Tokenized Equity Mapping

On April 10th, Binance Wallet launched its pre-IPO trading function. The first batch of assets includes: SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, Anduril, XAI, etc. Users can access Binance Wallet, select the pre-IPO trading tab, and immediately buy and sell without any restrictions.

This belongs to the SPV + Tokenization (Tokenized Equity Mapping) model, where equity is held by a third party (PreStocks) and then mapped 1:1 to tokens. It involves a real asset mapping, but no direct equity, voting rights, or dividends; it is a derivative product existing in the form of tokenized economic rights. OKX: RWA Infrastructure On April 17th, OKX Boost officially launched the Boost Pre-IPO Cooperation Program. As a non-custodial platform, OKX Wallet aggregates Pre-IPO tokenized assets issued by third-party projects, providing users with on-chain access and DEX trading experience. OKX provides on-chain trading and liquidity but does not directly issue, underwrite, or guarantee any tokenized assets on the platform. It belongs to the aggregation + non-custodial + DEX liquidity (RWA infrastructure) model, meaning it does not participate in equity holding, does not design securities structures, only provides an entry point and trading environment, acting as the trading infrastructure for all assets. Gate.io: Futures Market On April 9th, Gate.io launched its Pre-IPO contract product, with SpaceX ($SPCX) as the first project. The subscription price was $590 USD, with a total supply of 33,900 units and an implied market capitalization of approximately $1.4 trillion USD. It supports both USDT and GUSD for subscription and leverage (1–10x). Users can directly use the platform's stablecoins for subscription, making the overall process closer to a primary over-the-counter (OTC) trading model. There is no actual equity ownership; it is entirely transaction-driven and has speculative characteristics, belonging to the Pre-IPO price futures market. Bitget: Compliant Securities On April 10th, Bitget launched IPO Prime, with its first product being preSPAX. Powered by Republic, IPO Prime uses a subscription model (Republic's OpenDeal Portal is an SEC-registered crowdfunding portal; OpenDeal Broker (Capital R) is a FINRA-registered broker-dealer; Republic Capital Adviser is an SEC-registered investment advisor (RIA); and affiliated company Brassica Services is an SEC-registered transfer agent). Eligible users can apply to participate in tokenized issuances. It's a compliant securities structure (similar to structured notes), combining IPO subscriptions with structured returns, but it's not truly free on-chain trading. It's more like a Web3 gateway to traditional securities. Hyperliquid: Decentralized Derivatives Hyperliquid's pre-IPO product is called Ventuals (also commonly known as Hyperps), a perpetual contract for pre-IPO company valuation based on the HIP-3 permissionless market standard. The launch of vHYPE staking vault on October 24, 2025, is a landmark event for Hyperps. It belongs to the on-chain perpetual contract (Perp) + expected trading model. It does not involve real equity, SPV or securities structure, and is purely market-based pricing. It supports high leverage, high liquidity, on-chain matching, no asset anchoring, and relies entirely on market expectations. It is a decentralized version of the pre-IPO price game market. StableStock: Cross-border Financial Infrastructure On March 17th, StableStock announced the launch of Hong Kong stock trading and IPO subscription functions, supporting participation in Hong Kong IPO subscriptions with stablecoins. The partner is licensed brokerage HabitTrade (providing a compliant IPO subscription channel), supporting mainstream stablecoins such as USDT/USDC. III. The Impact of Pre-IPO On-Chain 1. Pre-IPO Marketization is Restructuring the Primary Market The on-chaining of Pre-IPOs by various trading platforms presents the previously untradeable primary market to ordinary investors. 1) From Locked Assets to Liquid Assets As mentioned above, after a Pre-IPO, investors need to wait for the company to successfully list and then sell their shares through the secondary market. Before listing, Pre-IPO assets lack liquidity. However, after being on-chained, users can trade at any time, with the market pricing the assets, thus enhancing liquidity.

2) From Institutional Pricing to User Pricing

In the traditional IPO system, pricing is entirely dominated by institutions. VCs conduct valuations, investment banks determine the issue price, and ordinary investors accept the price. However, in the on-chain Pre-IPO market, users can participate in trading, and liquidity, market sentiment, and other factors jointly determine the valuation. Pre-IPO has transformed from investment into trading.

3) From High-Net-Worth Individuals to Ordinary Investors

On-chain Pre-IPO allows ordinary investors to participate. The primary market will not only consist of VCs, PEs, and family offices, but will also show a trend towards retail investors in the future.

4) Improved Information Transparency

Information acquisition for traditional Pre-IPO investments relies on private placement channels, resulting in information asymmetry. After being on-chain, investors can clearly see transaction records, holdings, real-time price changes, and other data.

2. Exchanges are Squeezing Traditional VC Arbitrage Space

Exchanges have gone through three eras: spot trading, derivatives trading, and new coin issuance. Now they are moving towards the era of traditional financial asset pricing.

Exchanges are gradually replacing traditional venture capital (VC) firms, gaining control over initial public offerings (IPOs), pricing power, and liquidity. Exchanges are eroding the informational, investment, and lock-up advantages of traditional VCs, and arbitrage opportunities are being squeezed. Conclusion: Pre-IPO blockchain technology is gradually breaking the long-standing institutional-dominated capital allocation system. Lowering investment thresholds and increasing liquidity can quickly screen high-quality projects, but it also presents challenges such as securities compliance and regulatory issues, speculation due to price volatility, false liquidity due to insufficient trading depth, and issues related to project authenticity. The current pre-IPO trend presents an opportunity, but also harbors new risks.

data.title: The Spring of AI Overabundance – A Brief Reading of the Stanford 2026 AI Index
data.description:
One afternoon in April, I turned onto the Stanford Dish Trail from Sand Hill Road, intending to walk around and process what was on my mind. Halfway up the mountain, the rain started without warning—not a drizzle, but a short, heavy downpour typical of Bay Area spring. I didn't have an umbrella, so I simply stood under an oak tree, took out my phone, and opened the 300-plus-page report I hadn't finished reading that morning. The rain pounded loudly on the leaves. The ground beneath my feet turned from gray to dark brown. In the distance, the outline of Rosewood blurred into a beige in the rain. I read up to page forty, then looked up at Highway 280 below—car headlights moving slowly south, one after another. Suddenly, I realized this wasn't a report card. This is a map showing where humanity is heading—but no one has marked the intersections. I've decided not to read it chapter by chapter. I've decided to read it by the cracks. I. The First Crack: Ability Becomes Air Last year at this time, if you asked an AI to fix a complex code bug, it could do it correctly 60% of the time. This year, it's close to 100%. In one year, a complete professional skill went from a "scarce commodity" to a "default value." The abilities we've praised over the past decade—the ability to code, read papers, do math, and explain quantum physics—have collectively plummeted from their peak to the ground this year. A set of figures in this Stanford report made me stop and stare for a long time: 88% of organizations worldwide are already using AI, and four-fifths of college students use generative AI daily. What does this mean? It means that those "abilities" you were once so proud of, others can do too, and they can do them for free, instantly, and tirelessly. So what has become scarce? Not "what you can do." It's "knowing what to do." When everyone has something, it ceases to be a bargaining chip. The real bargaining chip has retreated upstream—to the intention itself. What do you want to do, why do you do it, are you willing to invest your time and energy in it, and are you willing to continue without external rewards? I've seen too many people fall into a new kind of anxiety in the past year: not "I can't," but "AI can do what I can, and it's cheaper than me." The outlet for this anxiety isn't downstream—not "I need to learn a newer skill." The outlet is upstream—in "What kind of person do I really want to become, and where do I really want my life to go?" It's not AI that's eliminating you. AI is forcing those who have never thought about "what I want" to confront this question for the first time. This is the first step: It's not that ability is depreciating, but that scarcity has changed its position. It has shifted from "can do" to "want to do." It has shifted from a verb to a motivation. Second, the second crack: Two giants side by side, but the supply chain hangs on a single needle. The report states that the gap between Chinese and American AI models is almost zero. Two giants were locked in a fierce battle at the forefront, with the lead shifting monthly—the US led at the beginning of the year, China's DeepSeek caught up in February, the US regained the lead in the summer, and was overtaken again in the fall. By March 2026, the top-tier US model was only leading by 2.7%. This is news. But not a story. The story lies on the next page of the same report: almost all of the most powerful AI chips come from a single Taiwanese foundry. Imagine this: two boxers are locked in a fierce battle in the ring, the world's media reporting on every punch—but no one notices that they share the same blood vessel for their hearts. This isn't a technical problem. It's that the causal chain of the entire civilization has been stretched too long and too thin, so thin that if any one node breaks, the whole system shuts down. We're used to talking about "decentralization." But real-world decentralization is never about "having many nodes." True decentralization is: if any one node disappears, the system can still function. By this standard, AI civilization today is a false form of decentralization. There are many models, many companies, many countries, but dig three layers down, and all the diversity converges to the same place. The US has 5,427 data centers this year, more than ten times that of the second-ranked country. This number sounds like a win. But from another perspective, it's also a concentration of risk—energy, cooling, land, talent, and chips are all crammed into the same geographical location. The more a civilization concentrates its essential resources on a few key points, the more fragile that civilization becomes. This is the second beat: We think we're building a network, but we're actually building a series circuit. One light goes out, the whole road goes dark. III. The Third Crack: Inconsistency—An IMO Gold Medalist, Yet Unable to Read a Clock Accurately. This is the page in the entire report that made me sit up straight the most. AI won a gold medal at the International Mathematical Olympiad. The same AI, looking at a round clock on a wall, has only a 50% probability of telling the time correctly. My first reaction upon reading this was to laugh. My second reaction was to remain silent. My third reaction was—we are all using the wrong ruler to measure this. Humans are trained from childhood to believe that intelligence is a linear concept. Scoring 90 points is smarter than scoring 70. A PhD is smarter than a Master's student. An IQ of 140 is smarter than 120. Someone who goes to Harvard is smarter than someone who goes to a state university. So we use this yardstick to measure AI: Has it surpassed me? When will it surpass me? Will it replace me? But AI, through its own growth, tells you: This yardstick has always been wrong. Intelligence isn't a line. Intelligence is a terrain. In some places, it's higher than three mountains—like the International Mathematical Olympiad, large-scale literature synthesis, or instantly accessing twenty languages. In other places, it's still at the foot of the mountain—like reading a simulated clock, judging whether a stranger is lying, or sensing whether a silence is awkward or intimate. Standing at its foot, you might think you've won. Looking up at its summit, you might think you've lost. But you're not even comparing on the same level. The real question isn't "Is it better than humans?" The real question is: In which areas am I irreplaceable? Everyone has to answer this question themselves. It's not in the report. No report can. But I want to add: the more you train only one "standardized skill," the more your strengths overlap with AI's, and the easier it is to be replaced. The more you cultivate those "inexplicable abilities"—intuition about people, sensitivity to beauty, awareness of the environment, and steadfastness in long-term commitment—the more you stand at its foot. The uneven terrain of AI, in turn, defines the irreplaceable area of ​​humanity. This is a gift for everyone, but few people open it. Fourth, the fourth crack: Accidents are rising, but trust hasn't kept up. Last year there were 233 AI-related accidents, this year there are 362. That's a 55% increase in one year. Almost all companies are competing on "how powerful my model is." Almost no company is seriously competing on "how reliable my model is." There's a very sobering statement in the report: Almost all developers seriously report on the capabilities of cutting-edge models; but reports on the benchmarks for responsible AI are fragmented. Even more chilling is this line: Researchers have found that improving one dimension of responsibility (such as safety) often reduces another dimension (such as accuracy). What does this mean? It means that we are no longer "making better AI," we are "making stronger AI." Stronger and better are two separate words. Strength is a one-dimensional thing, something you can compete for, something you can press release. Goodness is multi-dimensional, something that requires balance, something that's always in compromise. The entire industry has been running on the path of strength for the past year. Nobody's really running that path well. Because people who run that path well can't press releases—"Our model is safer but a little dumber" won't make headlines on Hacker News. This is the fourth beat: capability has outpaced trust. This isn't a moral judgment. It's an economic fact. Because humans won't use something they don't trust for long—they use it in the short term, then withdraw in the long term. All the AI ​​companies that rely on "amazing" features to retain users today will find themselves losing real users ten years from now. Those that remain are the companies that have made "reliability" the core of their products from day one. Trust is not a fence, trust is a moat. Not many companies understand this. Fifth, the fifth crack: Money comes, people leave. The US invests $285.9 billion in AI annually. China invests $12.4 billion. It sounds like the US has won big. Turn to the next page: The number of researchers and engineers wanting to work in AI in the US is 89% lower than in 2017. In just the past year, it has dropped by 80%. Money is flowing in. People are leaving. Money is a lagging signal. People are a leading signal. Money looks at performance, valuation, and rate of return over the past three years. People look at their quality of life over the next five years, visa stability, social atmosphere, and "Will I be treated well here?" I live near Sand Hill Road. Every time I drive past that road lined with venture capital logos, I'm reminded of something—the combined annual fundraising of every fund on this road couldn't buy a single top researcher who genuinely didn't want to come here. People leave because they sensed something was shifting—it could be visa policies, xenophobia, or that collective belief that "the future belongs to us." If a place can't retain its people, all the money it invests will eventually become a sunk cost. Because money can't create new ideas; ideas need living people. This is the section in the entire report I most want my friends in China to read, and also the section I most want my friends in Silicon Valley to read. To our friends in China: The brain drain from the US is not a victory for China. People leaving the US don't necessarily go to China. They might go to Singapore, the UAE, Canada, or anywhere they feel they can be treated well. The world's best minds are mobile today; they have choices. Don't understand this with a zero-sum mentality. To our friends in Silicon Valley: Don't understand why people leave in terms of "salary." Salary is the least important variable. People leave because of the atmosphere, the dignity, the feeling of "I have a future here." These things cannot be bought back with money. Once lost, it takes a generation to rebuild. Money can buy models, but it can't buy people who believe in a place.

VI. The Sixth Crack: The Fastest-Popularizing Technology Conceals the Quietest Value

Generative AI reached a 53% population penetration rate within three years. Faster than PCs, faster than the internet, faster than any technology in human history.

But interestingly: the highest penetration rate is not in the United States. Singapore is 61%, the UAE is 54%. The United States is only 28.3%, ranking 24th globally.

Even more interesting is this figure: the report estimates that generative AI creates $172 billion in value for US consumers annually—but this figure is not on any national economic report. Because it is free.

We are living in a very strange era: Real wealth is increasingly flowing through the free tier, while national statistics only show the paid tier. This means that if you measure your life by "income," you will become increasingly anxious because you have less and less to monetize. If you measure your life by "how much high-quality awareness I encounter each day, how many things I can accomplish that I couldn't do before," you will become increasingly wealthy. This isn't just motivational fluff. This is an era where statistics are outdated, and people need to change their dashboards. The old dashboard showed: house, car, savings, job. The new dashboard showed: how many kinds of intelligence you interacted with each day, how many things you accomplished with AI that were previously impossible, and what more important things you deepened in your free time. Neither dashboard was wrong. But those who only looked at the old dashboard felt a strange anxiety this spring—feeling they hadn't lost anything, yet they felt left behind. That's because true wealth lies on the other dashboard. VII. The Seventh Crack: Children Ahead of Schools Over 80% of American high school and college students use AI for homework. Only half of schools have AI policies. Of those, only 6% of teachers feel they understand their school's policies. To put it another way: Children are already living in a new world, while schools are still creating tests in the old world. This isn't an old story of "education lagging behind technology." It's a generational gap. It's not because children know more, but because they use AI to interact with the world every day, treating it as a partner they can ask questions of at any time; while teachers still define learning with "original assignments," "independent completion," and "no plagiarism." Two realities coexist. Both realities think the other is wrong. No one is building a bridge between them. If this bridge isn't built, ten years from now we'll see an entire generation learning in a way that was "self-taught without permission"—that's both a benefit of unbridled growth (this generation will be extremely precocious and extremely autonomous), and a trauma of being abandoned by the structure (they know the school is deceiving them, they know the teachers are pretending not to see, they know the "rules" are for those who believe in the rules). To parents: Your attitude towards your child using AI today isn't a question of "to manage or not to manage," it's a question of "whether you're willing to live with him in this new world." If you're not, he'll live on his own, he just won't tell you anymore. To those in education: What's truly worthwhile isn't "banning AI," nor "teaching AI," but rather, re-examining the meaning of "learning." If learning is simply "memorizing standard answers," then learning in the AI ​​era is dead. If learning is "constantly engaging in dialogue with high-quality awareness to develop one's own judgment," then learning in the AI ​​era has only just begun. The school system hasn't even figured out which side it wants to choose. The children have already chosen for it.

VIII. The Eighth Crack: Every Country Wants Its Own AI

The report reveals a quiet but important trend: developing countries are beginning to release their own national AI strategies. Supercomputing investment is rising globally. The proportion of contributors to open-source models from "non-US, non-European, and non-Chinese" countries is catching up with Europe and approaching that of the US.

This is a new story. For the past three years, the main narrative of AI has been "two major powers." Starting this year, it's "one hundred small countries trying not to be represented by the two major powers."

The essence of so-called "AI sovereignty" is not "I want to have my own model."

Its essence is—I don't want my people's language, history, feelings, and sense of humor to be defined by someone else's model. Imagine: if in the next ten years, everyone in the world spends three hours a day talking to AI, and these AIs are all trained on English corpora, designed with values ​​by a few Silicon Valley companies, and based on the default correctness of a certain culture—what will cultural diversity on Earth look like ten years from now? This isn't a technical issue. It's a civilization preservation issue. Like preserving languages, crafts, and biodiversity, humanity needs to protect the diversity of its own ways of thinking. That's why these countries, despite enormous resource pressures, are creating their own models. It's not about competing with the US and China. It's about ensuring that our next generation can still converse with AI using their own language sense. This is a slow but determined resistance. It won't make headlines, but it will shape the world for the next decade. Nine, the Ninth Crack: A Great River Separates Experts and the Public. 73% of AI experts believe AI will benefit employment. 23% of ordinary people think so. A difference of 50 percentage points. This 50 percentage point difference isn't about "who's right and who's wrong." These are two different worlds. Experts live upstream in the AI ​​field. They deal with cutting-edge models daily; they see possibilities, efficiency improvements, and things that were previously impossible suddenly becoming possible. Their lives are improving because of AI. Ordinary people live downstream in the AI ​​field. They see job postings requiring proficiency in AI tools, colleagues suddenly being laid off, their children's homework being indistinguishable between their own and AI's work, and scam calls increasingly sounding like their own family members. Their lives are becoming increasingly uncertain because of AI. People upstream say, "The river water is clear." People downstream say, "The water is rising." Both are telling the truth. Even more chilling is another set of data: Americans' trust in their government's regulation of AI is only 31%, the lowest in the survey. The most trusted regulator globally is not the US, not China, but the EU. This isn't a technical problem. It's a collapse of trust infrastructure. The more capable a country is, the less its own people trust it to manage that capability. Why? Because between capability and regulation, there needs to be something called an "institution." An institution is the law, a department, a procedure, and the promise that "someone will catch me if I fail." For the past two decades, American institutions have been continuously doubted, undermined, and ridiculed. With AI at this stage, the level of trust in institutions has reached its limit. This is the deepest crack in the entire report. Deeper than the cracks in models, chips, and education. Because models can be retrained, chips can be rebuilt, and education can be reformed—but once trust is shattered, it takes a generation to rebuild.

10. Look at the ten cracks together

Put these ten things on a piece of paper, and you'll see a pattern.

On one side, there's an exponential explosion of capabilities—models, data, investment, and adoption rates are all soaring. On the other side, the infrastructure to support these capabilities—trust, education, regulation, talent mobility, and cultural diversity—is cracking across the board.

We don't lack intelligence.What we lack is the container to catch that intelligence.

What is the container?

The container is everything that the phrase "I believe in you" is attached to: laws, schools, hospitals, neighborhoods, faith, contracts, promises between friends, and the tacit understanding between parents and children. These things are inherently fragile, and AI only makes them more fragile. Therefore, what this 2026 AI Index truly tells me is not "how much AI has progressed again." It's—in an era where capabilities are no longer scarce, the only scarce resource is the trust that carries those capabilities. Building models is no longer a moat. Doing things that people can trust is. This is the same instruction for everyone reading this—whether you're an entrepreneur, investor, teacher, parent, or just an ordinary user: Dedicate your life to the position of being "trustworthy." Because AI is taking on all other positions for you. The only thing it can't do is be "trustworthy." Because being trustworthy isn't a skill; it's a long-term, verifiable way of being, willing to bear the consequences. It can't be trained; it can only be lived out. This is the eleventh beat that wasn't mentioned in the report. It's also the sentence I thought of when I stood under that oak tree, as the rain gradually stopped. The rain has stopped. The air on the mountain is an order of magnitude fresher than when I came in. I put away my phone and continued walking towards the other end of the trail. In the distance, the headlights of cars on Sand Hill Road were already on, and the logos of various funds looked like tiny lighthouses in the twilight. Further away, the terrace at Rosewood was probably already full—a Negroni, a pitch deck, a conversation about the next billion dollars. The density of intelligence in the air was increasing. We can do more every day than last year, know more answers every day than last year, and mobilize a PhD-level brain for the price of a cup of coffee every day. But the trust between people—the kind of trust where "I entrust a part of myself to you, and I know you will receive it"—hasn't kept up. As I walked down the last slope, I suddenly realized: the most valuable things in the future are the oldest things. Not computing power. Not data. Not models. It's when one person says "I believe in you" to another, and that promise truly comes to fruition.

data.title: Russia Linked Crypto Exchange Grinex Halts Trading After $15 Million Wallet Hack Spreads Funds Across Tron and Ethereum
data.description:
Trading Suspended After Multi-Million Dollar Wallet Breach Hits Grinex

Crypto exchange Grinex has halted all trading activity after reporting the loss of more than 1 billion Russian rubles, roughly $13.7 million to $15 million, in a coordinated wallet breach.

The platform said funds were drained from 54 addresses before being rapidly moved across multiple blockchains.

Source: Grinex

The exchange confirmed that operations were frozen immediately after the incident, stating,

“All available information has been transferred to law enforcement agencies. A criminal complaint has been filed at the location of the infrastructure.”

The stolen assets were later tracked to a consolidation wallet holding around 45.9 million TRX, valued at nearly $15 million.

Sanctioned Russian exchange Grinex lost USD 15 million in a cyberattack on April 16.

TRM traced around 70 attacker-linked addresses — 16 more than Grinex publicly disclosed — with all known stolen funds consolidated at a single TRON address (~45.9 million TRX).

On-chain… pic.twitter.com/ky4sZFLCcj

— TRM Labs (@trmlabs) April 16, 2026

Foreign Intelligence Involvement Claimed But Evidence Remains Unclear

Grinex described the incident as highly sophisticated, suggesting it showed “an unprecedented level of resources and technology available only to entities of hostile states.”

The exchange alleged the attack was designed to disrupt Russia’s financial sovereignty and claimed possible involvement of foreign intelligence services.

However, no verified evidence has been presented to support those claims, and investigators have not confirmed the source of the breach.

The platform has previously stated it has faced repeated pressure linked to sanctions restrictions, blocked transactions and earlier minor cyberattacks, which it says created an ongoing hostile environment for operations.

How The Stolen Funds Were Moved Across Chains

Blockchain analysis firms tracking the incident reported that around $15 million in USDT was taken from Grinex wallets and quickly transferred through Tron and Ethereum networks.

The attackers then converted the stolen stablecoins into other assets, including TRX and ETH, a common tactic used to reduce the risk of freezing by stablecoin issuers.

Tether can block USDT linked to illicit activity, which likely prompted the rapid asset swaps.

Investigators also noted chain-hopping behaviour, with funds split across multiple wallets before being consolidated into a single address holding tens of millions in TRX.

Grinex says it suspended withdrawals + trading after a wallet infrastructure attack.

Elliptic estimates ~$15M USDT drained and then converted into TRX/ETH (likely to reduce Tether-freeze risk).#DeFiSecurity#CryptoHack

— SolidityScan (@SolidityScan) April 17, 2026

Wider Exposure Across Linked Exchanges Raises Questions

The breach may not have been isolated.

Blockchain intelligence reports suggest that wallets connected to TokenSpot, a Kyrgyzstan-based exchange with on-chain links to Grinex, also interacted with the same consolidation address used in the attack.

TokenSpot recorded a brief platform outage around the same period, later restoring services, while analysts flagged at least 16 additional addresses tied to the incident beyond those initially disclosed.

This overlap has raised concerns about shared infrastructure or liquidity flows between regional exchanges operating in closely connected ecosystems.

Sanctions Pressure And The Role Of A7A5 Under Scrutiny

Grinex has been widely associated with the now-sanctioned Garantex exchange, which was previously shut down over alleged links to sanctions evasion and illicit finance flows.

Grinex has been viewed by some analysts as its successor platform.

The exchange is also a key trading venue for A7A5, a ruble-backed stablecoin that has reportedly processed over $100 billion in transfers.

💵 Garantex returns as Grinex?
TRM Labs suspects rebrand after $100B laundering scandal.
Same team, same users, new name, new token (A7A5).#CryptoCrime#SanctionsEvasion#CryptoNews#Darkweb#Deepweb
More breaking news from the world and the Darkweb here: https://t.co/ZF7G3lwjoepic.twitter.com/0yf8ACXwdc

— Deepweb (@godeepweb) April 30, 2025

Critics, including blockchain researchers, have linked the token to attempts to bypass financial restrictions.

The European Union is also preparing wider measures targeting crypto transactions linked to Russia, focusing on networks associated with Garantex, A7A5 and related payment systems rather than individual platforms.

Rising Security Risks For Centralised Crypto Platforms

The incident adds to a growing list of large-scale exchange breaches in 2025 and 2026, where attackers have increasingly targeted hot wallets and signing systems.

With funds held in centralised wallets, platforms like Grinex remain exposed to rapid, high-volume thefts once access is compromised.

The speed at which stolen assets were moved across chains highlights how quickly attackers can obscure traces before enforcement action begins.

data.title: Web3 Compliance: Licensing Strategies for Stablecoin Payment Projects
data.description:
The invention of the steam engine didn't reduce coal consumption; instead, it expanded its applications. Similarly, the invention of the ATM didn't replace bank tellers; instead, it allowed bank branches to expand into more communities. This is the Jevons Paradox—as technology lowers unit costs, demand is released from those previously squeezed out by price. Stablecoin payments follow the same path: one serves as a supplement to the cross-border payment market, and the other connects 1.4 billion people worldwide who lack banking services to the financial network for the first time. Faced with such a global market like Day1Global, the question arises: what kind of license do I need to launch a stablecoin payment project? Therefore, this article will use my years of experience in applying for licenses and practical application (I have basically worked on or participated in all stablecoin payment + on-chain finance projects) to outline the compliance and licensing strategies for stablecoin payment projects. I. Compliance is not the same as licensing. When you see news reports of companies obtaining MiCA CASP or MAS MPI application announcements, these naturally become your reference points. However, for most early-stage projects, what others have achieved is not necessarily what you are starting from. "How do I obtain a license in compliance with regulations?" is not a question that can be answered directly. Before answering, let's break down three often-overlooked prerequisites: First, clarify that your business model requires this level of license—but stablecoin payments aren't a single track; acquiring, payment collection, crypto cards, OTC, and stablecoin issuance each have completely different regulatory frameworks. Second, estimate whether your business scale can afford the compliance costs of this level—MiCA CASP requires a total investment starting in the millions of dollars, and MAS MPI is on the same scale, which most early-stage projects cannot afford. Third, ensure compliance budget is in place, the team is ready, and funding channels are aligned—if any one of these three points is missing, even the most expensive license won't be viable. These three premises actually share the same distinction: A compliance posture and a compliance license are two different things. The former—KYC processes, AML monitoring, Travel Rule data fields, and auditability—must be built into the product architecture from day one; there's no room for negotiation. The latter—which license to obtain and when to upgrade—is a decision made progressively with the scale of the business. Of course, exceptions exist. If you have ample financial backing, or if you're serving highly regulated institutional counterparties from day one, or engaging in heavily regulated businesses like stablecoin issuance, then directly acquiring a Tier 1 license like MiCA or CASP is reasonable. However, for most early-stage projects that need to survive first, compliance decisions are a constrained optimization problem. Posture cannot be spared, but licenses can wait. II. Five Dimensions of Compliance and Licensing Decisions How to wait for a license? Compliance decisions are never a single-variable optimization. First, the business model. Payment isn't a single sector, but a whole combination. Acquiring, receiving, crypto cards, cross-border settlement, OTC, and stablecoin issuance each have completely different regulatory frameworks. A company with the same annual revenue of $10 million might need EMI/PI/MSO + MSB/SRO for B2B cross-border settlement, while needing Visa/Mastercard for B2C crypto cards—the two systems have almost no overlap. Furthermore, stablecoin payments almost never involve pure stablecoin businesses—fiat currency on/off-ramp is always a complementary arrangement. All licensing combinations discussed in this article implicitly include the fiat currency license (EMI, PI, MTL). Second, business volume. Look at TPV (Total Payment Volume, annual transaction volume) and take rate %. The take rate for B2B cross-border settlements is typically 0.1-0.5%, for consumer encrypted cards 1-2%, and for OTC matching 0.1-0.5%—the same TPV can generate ten times more revenue under different business models. Third, compliance costs. It's not just application fees and legal fees. The substantive costs of local offices, compliance personnel, and directors, plus the ongoing costs of annual audits, regulatory reports, and compliance technology stacks, can add up to several times the application fee. Fourth, funding channels. Obtaining a license does not guarantee the ability to open an account. Each license has a different funding path; before choosing a license, you must confirm that the path is feasible. This is the most likely point to stall business operations. Fifth, time. MSB: 3-4 months, SRO: 6-9 months, MiCA CASP: 12-18 months. Time is not an independent dimension; it's a constraint that runs through the previous four dimensions—business model determines which license you're waiting for, scale and cost determine how long you can sustain compliance spending, and access determines how long it takes to actually scale up operations after obtaining the license. Moreover, some time windows are rigid: the MiCA transition period expires on July 1, 2026, after which CASPs without MiCA authorization cannot operate in the EU. "A license can be waited for" doesn't mean you can wait indefinitely. Only by overlaying the timeline can you accurately position yourself on this map.

III. A Quick Overview of Mainstream Stablecoin Payment Licenses

The higher the tier, the wider the range of counterparties are willing to cooperate with you, but the annual compliance costs also jump by orders of magnitude.

（https://www.finma.ch/en/authorisation/self-regulatory-organisations-sros/）

IV. Three Stages of Compliance and Licensing: Minimization → Credit Enhancement → Institutional Level

Transform "Business Model × Looking at the five dimensions of "TPV × Compliance Cost × Funding Channel × Time" on a timeline, the compliance and licensing path for early-stage projects is not a one-time decision, but a progression of three stages. The first stage: Minimize compliance, get the business running first. The goal is straightforward: to get the business running legally. You need "the most economical compliance entity + a usable funding channel partnership." The two most common paths are: A single license with broad business coverage + a funding channel partnership. A Canadian MSB is the standard configuration at this stage—FINTRAC registration, with an initial cost of tens of thousands of US dollars, has explicitly covered Dealing in Virtual Currency since 2020. After obtaining the MSB, using a crypto-friendly PSP as a funding channel can support basic operations. Two lightweight licenses allow for geographical and business division. The combination of a Canadian MSB and a Hong Kong MSO can basically meet most business needs—the MSB handles stablecoin business, and the MSO handles cross-border fiat currency transactions in Asia; the two licenses complement each other. The most important thing at this stage is to quickly get your business up and running, suitable for early-stage projects validating PMF, or for players whose business model itself does not require a high-level license. The cost is profit sharing (the channel provider takes 0.3-1%) and business dependence on partner strategies. Regulatory oversight is also tightening—"outsourcing doesn't absolve you of regulatory responsibility" is an industry consensus; as the actual business entity, you still bear compliance responsibility. A Tier 1 license at this stage is not an asset, but a burden. For those with annual income below USD 1 million, avoid using regional licenses if possible.

（Rain Raises $250M to Expand Stablecoin Payments Infrastructure）

Rain has proven the logic of the first phase to its extreme.

Rain has proven the logic of the first phase to its extreme.

Rain has proven the logic of the first phase to its extreme.

Rain has proven the logic of the first phase to its extreme This company, which develops stablecoin payment card infrastructure, was founded in 2021. By early 2026, it had over 200 enterprise clients, an annualized transaction volume of $3 billion, and coverage in over 150 countries, including Western Union. However, its compliance architecture at this stage primarily consisted of multi-state MTL status in the US plus Visa Principal Membership—not relying on MiCA or MAS MPI. It wasn't until January 2026, after completing a $250 million Series C funding round with a valuation of $1.95 billion, that Rain explicitly included "expanding licensed markets" in its funding announcement. A company with an annualized transaction volume of $3 billion and a valuation of $2 billion only considered acquiring regional licenses after its Series C funding round. Phase Two: Credit Enhancement and Endorsement After Business Scale Up You're not just "making the business run legally," you're "making larger competitors willing to cooperate with you." When TPV reaches USD 500 million to 2 billion and annual revenue is USD 5 million to 15 million, the bottleneck is usually not whether you have enough licenses, but whether the signal strength of your competitors' due diligence is sufficient. When European banks, stablecoin issuers, and TradeFi institutions approach you, their compliance teams will require you to provide regulatory endorsements that are "more serious than MSB." Tier 3 licenses will be questioned in this kind of due diligence—not because of non-compliance, but because the signal strength of the "registration system" is insufficient. The standard practice is to also obtain a Tier 2 license. The most natural upgrade path is from a Canadian MSB to a Swiss SRO (VQF)—the business coverage is the same, but the counterparty's signal level is one tier higher, with upfront costs of hundreds of thousands of dollars and annual operating costs in the same range. SRO is currently a highly recognized self-regulatory license in the European crypto regulatory landscape, covering both stablecoins and fiat currencies. If paired with an EMI or PI for fiat currencies, it can meet the due diligence requirements of European institutional counterparties and enable European fiat currency business. Singapore's DTCPay follows this standard path. This stablecoin payment company previously held licenses in Singapore, Hong Kong, Australia, the United States, and Canada—covering multiple licenses across the Asia-Pacific region. In July 2025, it obtained a Green Light Letter from Luxembourg's CSSF, and in October, it officially received its EMI License, opening a passport entry point to the European market (EEA 30 countries, 450 million people). By March 2026, when the USD 10 million Series A tranche is completed, European market access will be in place. Obtaining a credit enhancement license first, then leveraging compliance milestones to secure the next round of financing—this is another strategy of "using Tier 2 licenses as leverage after business scales up." OKX obtained a PI License from the Malta MFSA in February 2026, a similar but more passive approach. OKX had already obtained a Malta MiCA License in January 2025—allowing it to legally conduct stablecoin trading and holding. However, the need for a PI license this time stems from its development of OKX Pay and OKX Card, which involve the on/off ramp of European fiat currency. Under the MiCA + PSD2 dual framework, stablecoin payments involving stablecoins (defined by EU law as Electronic Money Tokens, EMT) require an additional PI or EMI license. Even a company the size of OKX cannot avoid this. This path is unavoidable for all players wanting to engage in European stablecoin payments. At this stage, compliance is not about "pursuing the highest standards," but about "matching counterparty expectations and market access requirements." The third stage: Institutional-level licenses. When you have an annual income of over USD 15 million and want to build yourself into a "financial infrastructure" entity, compliance decisions enter the third stage—EU MiCA CASP, Abu Dhabi ADGM FSP, Singapore MAS MPI, and Switzerland PII / Crypto-Institution. Initial investment starts at millions of dollars, with annual operations reaching several million dollars. However, this level of license is not simply a "more expensive version of SRO"; it operates under a completely different regulatory logic. MiCA and MAS MPI require capital adequacy, bankruptcy remoteness, fiduciary duty, independent directors, internal audit, and DORA compliance—comparing your operational experience, the maturity of your compliance team, the robustness of your IT systems, and the professional background of key personnel. Even if you have money, if your team lacks licensed operating experience, your IT system hasn't undergone regulatory-level stress testing, and your compliance system isn't well-established—the regulators won't issue you a license, and even if they did, you wouldn't be able to sustain it. Circle serves as a reference point.
