As of early 2026, it held licenses at different levels in nine jurisdictions, from US state-level MTLs to French EMIs, UK FCA EMIs, Singapore MAS MPIs, Abu Dhabi ADGM FSPs, and Bermuda BMA Class F—this wasn't achieved in a single application; it was built up over 10 years, starting with a state-level MTL in 2014.

(Cirlce)

Why can't you skip the first two stages?

Regulators won't issue you a license. Regulators approve of a gradual path.

The regulators won't issue you a license. Regulators approve of a gradual path.

In the application review process for Tier 1 licenses such as MiCA, MAS MPI, and ADGM FSP, "fit and proper" is the core element. Regulators look at your compliance operation record, the maturity of your risk control system, the robustness of your IT system, and the work experience of key personnel. A company starting from scratch applying directly for MiCA CASP or MAS MPI has an extremely low success rate—regulators prefer a "gradual compliance upgrade" trajectory, not a "leapfrog" approach. The regulatory logic behind this is actually quite simple and completely consistent with traditional finance. Traditional banking licenses are issued in the same way; no one can skip payment licenses and trust licenses to directly obtain a banking license. The cost of skipping levels isn't just about application rejection. Even if regulators issue you a higher-level license for strategic reasons, you might not be able to meet the corresponding substantive compliance requirements. The size of the compliance team, the investment in IT systems, and the maturity of the risk control system required by MiCA are levels that early-stage projects cannot achieve from scratch within 18 months. Licenses can be bought, but capabilities cannot. However, there is another path: directly acquiring a licensed company with operational experience—Paxos's 2024 acquisition of Finland's EMI Membrane Finance follows this logic, granting EU EMI + MiCA compliance upon completion of the acquisition. Acquisitions bring both licenses and capabilities, but this requires sufficient funding and integration capabilities, which is not an option for early-stage projects. The three stages seem clear, but the costs of mismatch are severe. First, there's excessive compliance. Having secured seed funding, some feel they should "align with the leaders" and jump directly to the third stage, applying for MiCA CASP or MAS MPI. Eighteen months later, compliance costs have burned through $1 million, the business hasn't been fully validated, and they can't tell a compelling story for their Series A funding. This is a common mismatch in early-stage projects: using compliance budgets allocated to the SoV (Social Security Entity) pathway for MoE (Monetary Equivalent) business. Secondly, it's betting on the wrong market. In 2024-2025, many projects bet on a single Polish VASP (Visa Application Service) or Australian DCE (Digital Certificate of Entry) license, hoping to enter the EU or Australian market via passport. However, changes in regulatory pathways disrupted all expectations. The risks of relying on a single license will only increase in an era of fragmented regulation—compliance practices in 2026 have shifted from "optimization of a single jurisdiction" to a "combined compliance architecture." Thirdly, it's facing obstacles in accessing banking channels. This is the least discussed part of the compliance discussion. A license only defines "what you are allowed to do," while bank channels determine "whether you can actually conduct business." It's common for Canadian banks to refuse to open accounts for crypto MSBs; many early-stage projects that obtain licenses cannot open accounts, rendering their entire structure useless. However, this is highly case-by-case—two companies holding Canadian MSB licenses might have vastly different outcomes: one can open EMI channels like BVNK and ClearJunction, while the other might not. The difference lies in the UBO structure, business model, KYC documentation, and even luck. The most common risk control triggers in UBO structures: Chinese mainland passport holders as ultimate beneficiaries – most European EMIs have explicit or implicit rejection policies. Multi-layered offshore structures (BVI → Cayman → operating entity) – triggering enhanced due diligence or even direct rejection. UBOs with past associations with cryptocurrency exchanges – common reasons for rejection. High-risk keywords in the business model description: "OTC" in the onboarding file almost automatically triggers manual review. "peer-to-peer payments", "unhosted wallet", and "self-custodied wallet" also trigger manual review. "aggregator" and "crypto mixing" directly shut down most EMI channels. "gaming" + "crypto" The combination of these elements—equivalent to a high-risk label in the current environment—is a direct rejection. Geographical dimension: Any description of nodes involving Iran, Russia, or North Korea in the transaction path is a direct disqualification. The danger of these trigger points lies in the fact that they don't appear in the rejection letter. The standard response from EMI is "not a good fit at this time," and you never know which word will land you in the dust. Choosing a bank channel cannot be done by "following a map." Due diligence must be conducted in advance—and whether an account can be opened ultimately depends on the nature of your business and regulatory classification. VI. MoE or SoV: The Switch That Determines All Costs

Why these three stages? Why is the compliance cost of each stage an order of magnitude different?

There is an underlying shift behind this: the role of stablecoins in stablecoin payments is shifting from "medium of exchange" to "store of value."

This shift determines the order of magnitude of your compliance costs.

  • MoE Phase—Stablecoins are merely a conduit for funds. Users exchange fiat currency for USDT to spend immediately, with the holding time measured in hours or days. Regulators focus on "pass-through" requirements such as AML and Travel Rules. Licenses like the Canadian MSB and Hong Kong MSO are designed around MoE, with low barriers to entry. The SoV phase begins with users holding stablecoins as assets, with holding periods increasing from days to months and years. At this point, you are no longer a conduit, but an asset holder. Regulatory focus shifts to reserve management, bankruptcy remoteness, fiduciary duty, and interest rate compliance—each requiring a higher-level license. The SoV transition is expected to become a global regulatory consensus in 2025-2026. The US GENIUS Act (signed in July 2025) defines stablecoins as "payment stablecoins," restricting issuance rights to OCC-regulated issuers or banks with special licenses—Stripe's Bridge has already obtained OCC Initial Trust Bank Approval, and Circle, Ripple, Paxos, and Fidelity are in the queue. Hong Kong's HKMA Stablecoin Ordinance (effective August 2025) and the issuance of the first batch of issuance licenses in March 2026 require capital adequacy ratios and reserve management at the same level as banks. The regulator has already included stablecoin issuance at the "financial infrastructure" level—the regulatory path directly leads to a banking license or a quasi-banking license. The implications for early-stage projects are clear: when designing their business models, they need to clearly define whether they are a MoE (Money Market Asset) or a SoV (Socially Oriented Virtual Asset), or both, or whether they will achieve this through DeFi. The compliance costs of the two paths differ by an order of magnitude. A MoE's budget for SoV business will never be sufficient. Of course, MoE and SoV are not mutually exclusive switches. Many projects start with MoE, but as users begin to keep balances in their wallets and hold them for longer periods, such as days to months, their businesses will naturally shift towards SoV. This evolution itself isn't the problem—the problem is whether your compliance architecture has upgraded along with your business model. The three-stage progressive logic is essentially a response to this evolution. Knowing which path you're on is crucial to understanding how to handle licenses. VII. Returning to the First Question "Which license should I get?"—This is the question posed at the beginning of this article. After exploring the five dimensions, three stages, three types of mismatches, and the underlying logic of MoE/SoV, this question naturally evolves into a set of more specific judgments: What stage is my business currently in? What do my competitors want? Are banks willing to open an account for me? Am I on the path to MoE or SoV? Match your scale and upgrade in stages. Licenses are a follower indicator for business, not a leading indicator. First, get the business running.

data.title: Are humans being replaced by robots in the crypto world?
data.description:
Author: blocmates   Translator: Luffy, Foresight News

If you've been in the crypto industry for the past few years, like us, you've clearly felt that the industry atmosphere has completely changed. Market excitement has faded considerably, even becoming somewhat subdued. Currently, the only things that seem to capture people's interest are things related to artificial intelligence and intelligent agents.

It's widely believed that this is because the crypto industry is heavily optimizing to accommodate AI intelligent agents, while the "human layer," still relying on direct human interaction and working on the front lines, is gradually being marginalized.

So from a human perspective, the industry seems dull; but the on-chain ecosystem remains active on a completely new level—the intelligent agent layer. Increased efficiency is prompting more users to shift to AI-driven interaction methods; these platforms, once designed for human clicks, are now optimized for non-humans.

In February of this year, Uniswap Labs, a leading project in the industry, launched seven open-source AI agent skills, enabling autonomous AI-coded agents such as Claude and Cursor to interact directly and stably with the on-chain Uniswap protocol. However, contrary to the widespread rhetoric on social media that "AI agents will devour everything," a closer look at the data reveals that the growth of agent activity is limited to niche areas rather than an industry-wide explosion. We decided to delve deeper to see which areas have already been dominated by agents and which remain human-dominated, ultimately answering the question: Is the human layer in the crypto world truly dying out? We will also review solutions built on new infrastructure that ensure human control is not lost. AI-Driven Domination: In some niche sectors, AI-driven activities have become dominant, with human interaction significantly decreasing. Typical examples include: Derivatives Trading: The perpetual contract market is the most typical example of a highly liquid market dominated by robots in the cryptocurrency industry. Speed, pattern recognition, and 24/7 uninterrupted trading are capabilities that machines far surpass humans in; no one would argue that humans should manually rush into trading. The top ten perpetual contract protocols have a combined trading volume of $500.6 billion in the past 30 days, with Hyperliquid alone accounting for $189 billion (38%), followed by edgeX ($70.6 billion) and Aster ($64.7 billion). Aster once held a two-week, high-volatility real-time trading competition, "Human vs. AI," with highly representative results: 43% of human participants were eliminated, while all 30 AI agents successfully completed the competition with no eliminations, achieving a 100% survival rate. The overall return on investment for the human trading team was -32.22%, compared to -4.48% for the AI ​​agents.

Arbitrage Trading

This is the area in the crypto industry most thoroughly dominated by bots. Large-scale, profitable human MEV operators are virtually nonexistent (Note: MEV refers to Maximum Extractable Value, a strategy that earns additional profits beyond standard block rewards and transaction fees by adding, deleting, or reordering transactions when creating new blocks). The public chain MEV ecosystem has evolved into a highly competitive automated trading industry, with professional bots and infrastructure continuously scanning on-chain mempools.

Lending is a typical layered automated scenario. As mentioned earlier, liquidation bots have deeply penetrated the market, but the vast majority of deposit and lending decisions are still made by humans.

Leading protocols such as Aave V3 and Morpho V1 have huge TVL (TVL), while the redeployment TVL of DeFAI agents in lending and yield farming exceeds $2 billion, but it only accounts for less than 2% of the total TVL of DeFi ($130-140 billion).

This clearly shows that core aspects such as deposit decisions, collateral selection, and risk appetite are still dominated by humans.

AI agents only handle peripheral processes; core control remains in human hands. As of March 2026, the total market capitalization of stablecoins was approximately $312 billion. Excluding bots, MEVs, and wash trading, the adjusted transaction volume of real economic activity reached $28 trillion in 2025, representing a CAGR of 133% since 2023. In August 2025, small-amount stablecoin transactions under $250 reached a record 5.84 billion, primarily used by humans for transferring money to friends and family, paying freelancers, and splitting bills. Over 80% of USD stablecoin transactions occurred outside the US, where AI agent penetration is relatively low. Emerging market users use stablecoins as a channel for the US dollar and as an economic hedging tool, directly forming the core foundation of the stablecoin market. In February 2026 alone, the monthly transaction volume reached $1.78 trillion. Meanwhile, clearer regulations have driven the explosion of crypto card products, allowing users to independently manage and use crypto assets in traditional card-swiping scenarios. In this field, intelligent agent participation is only about 5%, with the rest being entirely real human fund transfers, and many users are even unaware that they are using a crypto system. Wallets are the last layer of interface between humans and the blockchain that cannot be completely abstracted. Even with various simplified designs, the transaction authorization process still requires human supervision: someone must sign, and someone must determine whether the current operation is trusted. The entire wallet industry is focusing on human-centered upgrades: mnemonic-free account opening, human-readable transaction previews, biometrics, card-based payments, etc. By 2026, high-quality wallets had evolved from simple storage tools into complete financial dashboards. Enterprise-grade smart agent wallets, on the other hand, set limits, whitelists, audit logs, and emergency pauses, treating smart agents as restricted operators rather than fully authorized signers. Human and Smart Agent Verification Layer: The more smart agents there are, the more important this layer becomes. The more widespread the on-chain smart agent activity, the higher the value of proving "you are human" or "the smart agent is controlled by humans." The following projects are building the relevant infrastructure to prevent the world from being completely taken over by machines: World & AgentKit (formerly Worldcoin (WLD)) has verified over 17 million users through iris scanning Orb devices. The project aims to address the proliferation of AI in the world by building a truly human-centric digital infrastructure. The company launched AgentKit. This toolkit allows AI agents to carry cryptographic proofs that they are backed by a unique human through a World ID, and integrates with Coinbase and Cloudflare's x402 protocol for stablecoin micropayments. t54 builds a trust and security infrastructure for the agent economy, addressing the risks (lack of verification, lack of accountability, vulnerability to fraud, and non-compliance) when AI agents manage real funds. t54 uses an x402-secure trust layer for real-time risk scoring, detecting fraudulent activities such as hint injection, providing a security barrier for institutions and users. Self Protocol builds a decentralized zero-knowledge proof "human-agent binding layer" based on ERC-8004. Using ZK technology, each AI agent is anchored to a verified human owner, achieving proof of humanity while protecting privacy, preventing Sybil attacks, supporting self-custodied wallets and autonomous behavior, and maintaining human accountability. Kite AI, an L1 public chain designed specifically for the agent internet, provides agent passports (verifying identity, delegation, and programmable spending rules), autonomous stablecoin payments, governance, and verification capabilities, allowing agents to authenticate, trade, and collaborate without intermediaries. In conclusion, strictly speaking, we are not against agents. The data is clear: in areas such as trading, MEV, and yield optimization, robots have already won. The fact that 43% of humans suffered margin calls while agents suffered zero, demonstrates who reigns supreme in this "speed game." However, looking at the industry ecosystem as a whole, many aspects that truly impact daily life are still dominated by humans: payment, identity verification, and authentication. These are the levels where real value and income are generated, and they share the common characteristics of requiring judgment, trust, physical presence, or cultural context. We believe that in these areas and industries, project teams should not completely abandon the construction of human interaction mechanisms. At least at this stage, intelligent agents need humans far more than humans need intelligent agents. Teams that truly understand this, and projects that simultaneously build intelligent agents and authentication systems, deserve continued attention.

data.title: Two antidotes to quantum panic won't save Satoshi Nakamoto's cryptocurrency.
data.description:
Overnight, BTC continued to fluctuate around 75k, and there were various opinions in the market.

Two days ago, Jiaolian wrote a popular science article about two quantum solutions proposed by researchers—one called QSB, for backup; and the other called ZK proof, for emergency use[1]. At that time, Jiaolian's conclusion was: the quantum panic can stop, and the backup solution has been found.

But on April 16, Cardano founder Charles Hoskinson jumped out and poured cold water[4]. He said: Your BIP-361 proposal cannot save everyone. At least 1.7 million Bitcoins before 2013, including Satoshi Nakamoto's 1.1 million, will be permanently frozen.

This is a bit harsh.

However, after carefully reading his argument, ChainChain discovered that the problem he pointed out was precisely a blind spot that ChainChain's previous article hadn't had time to elaborate on—the emergency remedy of ZK proof has a fatal prerequisite: you need a BIP-39 mnemonic phrase. An ancient whale without a mnemonic phrase cannot prove whether it is the owner or the thief. Let's review: What do the two antidotes treat? ChainChain's previous article already explained this clearly; here's a brief recap. The quantum threat has two fatal flaws: Flaw 1: Public keys are exposed during transactions. Ordinary addresses store the hash of the public key, sealed like an envelope. But when you want to spend money, you have to tear open the envelope and reveal the public key. Quantum computers can slowly calculate from this exposed public key to deduce your private key. **Weakness Two:** Many addresses' public keys are already exposed on the blockchain. Early whales like Satoshi Nakamoto used P2PK format public key addresses, where the public key was written on the blockchain from the beginning without any protection. **Addressing Weakness One:** Researchers proposed the QSB scheme. Using opcodes like OP_CAT, the public key is hidden in a complex set of computational steps, verified, and then discarded without being written to the blockchain. This makes it impossible for quantum computers to access it. **Addressing Weakness Two:** Researchers proposed the ZK proof scheme. It gradually phases out old addresses through community consensus while providing a migration channel based on zero-knowledge proofs. Early adopters could prove ownership of a particular address and then recover their funds. At the time, the teaching chain stated that these two remedies—one a safety net, the other an emergency measure—neither required a hard fork, and the quantum panic could finally subside. However, one point needs clarification: QSB was not designed specifically for P2PK addresses. It addresses the issue of public keys being exposed during transactions, not those old addresses whose public keys were already exposed on-chain. Therefore, it's not true that QSB couldn't save Satoshi Nakamoto—it wasn't intended for this problem. But the teaching chain overlooked a more crucial point: ZK proofs aren't suitable for everyone.

ZK proof medicine is only for people with mnemonic phrases

Let me first explain what zero-knowledge proof is.

Simply put, it means that you can prove to the network that you know a certain secret, but you don't have to say the secret itself. For example, you walk into a bar, and the bartender says that you need to prove that you are a VIP member, but you can't show me your membership card. You make a set of gestures that only VIPs can know, and the bartender understands it at a glance.

The ZK proof scheme envisioned in the BIP-361 proposal uses the BIP-39 mnemonic phrase as the secret to be proven[4][5]. BIP-39 is a standard introduced in 2013. It encodes the seed of the private key into 12 or 24 English words. You only need to remember these words to recover the entire wallet. Moreover, from the mnemonic phrase to the private key, it goes through 2048 hash iterations of the PBKDF2 function—this process is irreversible, and even quantum computers cannot handle it. Therefore, the logic of BIP-361 is: Do you have a mnemonic phrase? If so, you can prove to the network that you know the mnemonic phrase through ZK proof, and then the network allows you to migrate your coins to a secure address. Don't have a mnemonic phrase? Then you can only prove ownership through traditional signatures. But if the quantum era comes, signing is suicide—once you sign, your public key is exposed, and a quantum computer can quickly calculate your private key and transfer the money before you do. This is what Hoskinson meant when he said: it's not possible[4]. The watershed moment of 2013 BIP-39 was introduced in 2013. Before that, Bitcoin wallets used a completely different key management method. At that time, a private key was just a private key. Users backed up a file called wallet.dat, which contained a bunch of randomly generated private keys. There was no mnemonic phrase and no standardized recovery mechanism[4][5]. Satoshi Nakamoto was mining between 2009 and 2010. He used the P2PK address format, and his public key was exposed; his private key was a directly generated random number without a corresponding BIP-39 mnemonic phrase. This forms an insurmountable watershed: private keys before 2013, without a mnemonic phrase, cannot be saved under BIP-361. Private keys with BIP-39 mnemonic phrases after 2013, with a mnemonic phrase, can be saved through ZK proof. Hoskinson estimates that there were about 1.7 million Bitcoins before 2013, of which Satoshi Nakamoto held about 1.1 million. These coins cannot be saved under the BIP-361 framework [4][8]. This isn't a technical oversight. It's a mathematical deadlock. The private key generation methods prior to 2013 are incompatible with BIP-39; there's no way to convert those old private keys into a BIP-39 mnemonic phrase (if it were possible, then a hacker who cracks the private key could too). The dilemma of raw private keys: unable to prove you're the owner. ChainChain believes the key point here is that the advent of quantum computers has precisely eliminated the only way for a raw private key holder to prove they are the owner. Traditionally, how do you prove you own a sum of Bitcoin? You sign with your private key. The network verifies the signature, and the money is yours. With the advent of quantum computers, this proof process becomes suicidal. Because signing exposes the public key, and a quantum computer can deduce the private key from the public key. The moment you sign, someone else can use your private key to transfer your money. The network cannot distinguish whether the signature was made by the owner or by a quantum hacker who just cracked the code. They are mathematically equivalent. So what can be done? Users in the BIP-39 era had an escape route: use ZK proofs to bypass the signature and directly prove they knew the mnemonic phrase. But users with bare private keys don't have this route. They can only choose to sign (suicide) or not sign (their coins are permanently frozen). This is what was mentioned earlier: you can't prove you're the owner or the thief. Satoshi Nakamoto's 1.1 million Bitcoins, if he were still alive and wanted to transfer them, he couldn't prove to the network that he was Satoshi Nakamoto and not a quantum hacker. His private key lacked a mnemonic phrase that could be used for ZK proofs. His public key was exposed, and his private key could be cracked, allowing the hacker to steal his coins—unless he could appear before quantum computers became a reality and transfer his coins in advance. Therefore, he either had to let his coins be permanently frozen, be robbed, or appear in the world. This is a triple dilemma, and Satoshi Nakamoto is trapped inside. How to view the blockchain

First, the technical problem Hoskinson mentioned does exist. Bitcoin before 2013 could not be protected under the BIP-361 framework. This is not a bug, but a mathematical fact that BIP-39 differs from the older system.

Second, but this does not mean Bitcoin is finished. The freezing of 1.7 million coins is equivalent to the indirect destruction of this supply, which is not necessarily a negative for the market. Moreover, Satoshi Nakamoto's coins have never been touched; no one knows whether he is still alive or has passed away. The permanent freezing of these coins may not have any real impact on the market.

Third, the conclusion in the previous article on ChainX that the quantum panic can subside still holds true. This is because ordinary users use BIP-39 wallets, which have mnemonic phrases and escape routes; otherwise, they have ample time to transfer assets before the advent of quantum computers. The QSB scheme also provides a fallback solution for scenarios requiring address reuse. Those truly trapped are the ancient whales from before 2013—including Satoshi Nakamoto. However, they are most likely no longer alive or have already lost their private keys. Being frozen is no different from losing them. Fourth, the lesson for ordinary people might be: ensure your wallet is generated based on a BIP-39 mnemonic phrase. If you're still printing your private key directly from bitaddress.org, or generating a raw private key using some outdated desktop wallet, you should probably migrate to a standard HD wallet as soon as possible. Write down 12 or 24 words on paper and lock it in a safe. On the day the quantum threat arrives, those with mnemonic phrases will have an escape route. Those with only raw private keys will be trapped in the prison of mathematics, unable to prove their identity.

---

References:

[1] Liu Jiaolian, “Two antidotes to quantum panic: one as a safety net, one as an emergency measure”, Apr 13, 2026. [link](/a/20260413)

[2] a16z crypto, “Quantum Computing and Blockchain: Separating Signal from Noise”, Jan 25, 2026. [Link](https://x.com/a16zcrypto/status/2015494412009086994)

[3] Adam Back, Twitter post, Jun 4, 2025. [Link](https://x.com/adam3us/status/2001977579428041134)

[4] Logan Hitchcock, "Quantum Proposal Won't Save Satoshi's Bitcoin, Says Cardano Founder Hoskinson", *Decrypt*, Apr 16, 2026. [Link](https://decrypt.co/364676/cardano-charles-hoskinson-bitcoin-quantum-debate)

[5] "Bitcoin's BIP-361 Quantum Fix Splits Community Over Address Freezing", *Gate News*, Apr 17, 2026. [Link](https://web.gate.it/tr/news/detail/bitcoins-bip-361-quantum-fix-splits-community-over-address-freezing-20370363)

[6] “Cardano founder: BIP-361 is actually a hard fork, or it freezes early BTC”, *ChainCatcher*, Apr 17, 2026. [Link](https://www.chaincatcher.com/en/article/2259171)

[7] “Hoskinson alerts Bitcoin enfrenta una crisis por riesgo cuántico y BIP 361”, *DiarioBitcoin*, Apr 15, 2026. [Link](https://www.diariobitcoin.com/seguridad/hoskinson-alerta-que-bitcoin-enfrenta-una-crisis-por-riesgo-cuantico-y-bip-361/)

[8] Cryptocurrency Fundamentals and Private Key Management: A Comprehensive Guide to Protecting from Elliptic Curves to Quantum Security, *Tencent Cloud*, Nov 18, 2025. [Link](https://cloud.tencent.cn/developer/article/2590422)
